title: What grit for walnut before oil finish?
problem: Walnut can show sanding scratches or look uneven after oil finish.
likely_cause: Oil finish highlights the scratch pattern, especially if coarse marks remain before finishing.
recommended_grit: Sand walnut through 180 and 220. Use 320 only for a lighter final touch on visible surfaces.
steps: Move to 220 and remove the previous scratch pattern.
Use 320 lightly only on visible face grain.
avoid: Do not stop at 120 grit before oil finish on walnut.
success_check: The walnut shows an even scratch pattern with no coarse lines before oil is applied.
quick_answer: For walnut before oil, sand 180 then 220. Use 320 lightly only on visible face grain.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip dust removal before oil.
Do not leave 120-grit scratches under an oil finish.
search_phrases: what grit for walnut before oil
sandpaper grit for walnut oil finish

title: What grit for mahogany before stain?
problem: Mahogany can show sanding scratches or uneven stain color.
likely_cause: Coarse scratches and uneven sanding absorb stain differently across the surface.
recommended_grit: Use 180 then 220 for most mahogany stain prep.
steps: Sand with the grain using 180 grit.
Move to 220 for final stain prep.
Clean dust from pores and edges.
Apply stain only after the surface looks evenly sanded.
avoid: Do not over-sand one area with fine grit and leave another area at a coarser scratch pattern.
success_check: The surface color and scratch pattern look even before stain.
quick_answer: For mahogany before stain, sand with 180, then finish with 220. Keep the scratch pattern even.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand across the grain.
Do not leave dust in open pores before staining.
search_phrases: what grit for mahogany before stain

title: What grit sequence for wood turning?
problem: Turned wood can show circular sanding lines or torn grain.
likely_cause: Lathe sanding can leave ring scratches if grits are skipped or sanding pressure is uneven.
recommended_grit: Use 120 or 150 for shaping marks, then 180, 220, 320, and 400.
steps: Start with 120 or 150 only if tool marks remain.
Inspect under side light before applying finish.
avoid: Do not skip from 150 directly to 400 on turned wood.
success_check: The turned surface has no visible ring scratches before finish.
quick_answer: For wood turning, use 120 or 150 only for tool marks, then 180, 220, 320, and 400.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not skip intermediate grits on curved turned surfaces.
Do not press hard in one narrow ring.

title: What grit for wooden toys?
problem: Wooden toys need smooth edges without over-rounding details.
likely_cause: Coarse sanding can leave scratches, while heavy pressure can change the shape of small parts.
recommended_grit: Use 180 for shaping cleanup, then 220 and 320 for smooth hand feel.
steps: Remove sharp edges lightly with 180 grit.
Remove all dust before applying a safe finish.
avoid: Do not use coarse grit aggressively on small toy parts.
success_check: The toy feels smooth by hand with no sharp splinters or deep scratches.
quick_answer: For wooden toys, use 180 for cleanup, then 220 and 320 for a smooth hand feel.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not round off important small details with heavy pressure.
Do not leave splinters before finishing.

title: What grit for picture frames before painting?
problem: Paint on picture frames can show old scratches or fail to grip glossy finish.
likely_cause: Small decorative surfaces need light scuff sanding without flattening details.
recommended_grit: Use 220 for scuff sanding and 320 for fine paint prep.
steps: Remove dust before primer or paint.
avoid: Do not use heavy pressure on decorative frame details.
success_check: The frame is clean, dull, and evenly scuffed before painting.
quick_answer: For picture frames before painting, scuff with 220, then use 320 lightly on visible areas.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand through thin decorative edges.
Do not paint over dust or gloss.
search_phrases: what grit for picture frames before painting

title: Cabinet door edges sand through too fast
problem: Cabinet door edges lose finish or expose bare spots faster than flat areas.
likely_cause: Edges have less surface area and sand through quickly when pressure is too heavy.
recommended_grit: Use 220 for edge scuffing and 320 for light between-coat sanding.
steps: Use very light pressure on edges.
Use 220 only enough to dull the gloss.
avoid: Do not bear down on cabinet door edges.
success_check: Edges are scuffed but not cut through to unwanted bare spots.
quick_answer: Use light pressure on cabinet door edges. Scuff with 220 and use 320 lightly between cured coats.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not treat edges like flat panels.
Do not use coarse grit on finished cabinet edges unless stripping.
search_phrases: what grit for cabinet door edges

title: Veneer edge chips while sanding
problem: Thin veneer edges chip, lift, or sand through during prep.
likely_cause: Veneer is thin and fragile. Coarse grit or sanding across an unsupported edge can break it.
recommended_grit: Use 220 or 320 with very light pressure. Avoid coarse grit on thin veneer edges.
steps: Support the veneer edge where possible.
Stop as soon as the edge feels smooth.
avoid: Do not use 80, 120, or heavy pressure on veneer edges.
success_check: The veneer edge is smooth without chips, lifting, or sand-through.
quick_answer: For veneer edges, use 220 or 320 with very light pressure. Do not use coarse grit.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand unsupported veneer edges aggressively.
Do not try to level veneer with coarse paper.

title: What grit removes water stains from wood?
problem: A water stain remains on wood after cleaning.
likely_cause: The stain may be in the finish or upper wood fibers, so sanding must be controlled to avoid a low spot.
recommended_grit: Start with 180 only on the stained area, then blend with 220 and 320.
steps: Sand the stained area lightly with 180.
avoid: Do not dig a hollow spot into the wood.
success_check: The stain is reduced and the repaired area blends smoothly with the surrounding wood.
quick_answer: For a wood water stain, sand lightly with 180, blend with 220, then finish with 320 before refinishing.
mistakes_to_avoid: Do not sand one small spot too deeply.

search_phrases: what grit removes water stains from wood

title: What grit between shellac coats?

avoid: Do not sand through shellac with heavy pressure.

title: What grit to scuff sand sanding sealer?

avoid: Do not sand soft varnish or sand through edges.
